coredata.dc:title: Application of sonochemically synthesized SnS and SnS2 in the electro-Fenton process: Kinetics and enhanced decolorization
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Matyszczak, Grzegorz
Fidler, Aleksandra
Polesiak, Emilia
Sobieska, Małgorzata
Morawiec, Krzysztof
Zajkowska, Wiktoria
Lawniczak-Jablonska, Krystyna
Kuzmiuk, Piotr
coredata.dc:description: SnS and SnS2 powders were synthesized with the use of ultrasound. The indirect sonication was applied with ultrasound frequency 40 kHz and acoustic power 38 W/L. Products of syntheses were examined with PXRD, TEM, EDX, XPS, and UV–Vis (the Tauc method) investigations. The resulting microparticles were used for tip coating of copper cathodes. These electrodes were used in the degradation of model azo-dye Metanil Yellow by the electro-Fenton process. The efficiencies of degradation using copper, SnS-coated copper, and SnS2-coated copper cathodes are compared. Kinetics of degradation of Metanil Yellow in the electro-Fenton process with the application of three different cathodes is also investigated. It was found that the degradation follows pseudo-first-order and that SnS-coated copper cathode improves the efficiency of degradation, while SnS2-coated copper cathode decreases the efficiency of degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electro-Fenton process
Metanil Yellow
Decolorization
Tin(II) sulfide
Tin(IV) sulfide
Sonochemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for the electro-Fenton process and monitoring of dye degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of the Metanil Yellow azo-dye molecule.

The STEM image of SnS powder. A red square indicates an area from which the EDX spectrum has been collected. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The STEM image of SnS2 powder. A red square indicates an area from which the EDX spectrum has been collected. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The high resolution XPS spectra: a) Sn 3d doublet for SnS particles, b) S 2p doublet for SnS, c) Sn 3d doublet for SnS2 particles with two components, d) S 2p doublet for SnS2.
Comparison of HR-TEM images (lower magnitude) of sonochemically synthesized SnS2 and SnS powders.
Comparison of HR-TEM images (higher magnitude) of sonochemically synthesized SnS2 and SnS powders.
Fast Fourier Transform images of areas 1 (a) and 2 (b) from Fig. 9a.

Upper row: on left, high-resolution image of SnS2 hexagonal nanocrystal with characteristic interplanar distance d(0 0 1) = 599 pm; on right, image of orthorhombic SnS nanocrystal with the zone axis [10–1] and interplanar distances d(0 4 0) = 285 pm and d(1 0 1) = 293 pm. Bottom row: the compositions of theoretical semi-rings with radiuses corresponding to the interplanar distances according to the XRD data, in comparison with Fourier transform of the high resolution images taken from the specimen region containing assembly of several dozen nanocrystals.
The Tauc plots of sonochemically synthesized SnS and SnS2 powders.
Transformed experimental data for reactions using copper, SnS-coated copper, and SnS2-coated copper cathode for three reactions.
Results of the EDX and XPS analysis of synthesized powders.
Observed kinetic constants for each experiment for each investigated cathode.
Mean efficiencies of the electro-Fenton process for three studied cathodes.

head.ce:title: Application of sonochemically synthesized SnS and SnS2 in the electro-Fenton process: Kinetics and enhanced decolorization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SnS and SnS2 powders were synthesized with the use of ultrasound. The indirect sonication was applied with ultrasound frequency 40 kHz and acoustic power 38 W/L. Products of syntheses were examined with PXRD, TEM, EDX, XPS, and UV–Vis (the Tauc method) investigations. The resulting microparticles were used for tip coating of copper cathodes. These electrodes were used in the degradation of model azo-dye Metanil Yellow by the electro-Fenton process. The efficiencies of degradation using copper, SnS-coated copper, and SnS2-coated copper cathodes are compared. Kinetics of degradation of Metanil Yellow in the electro-Fenton process with the application of three different cathodes is also investigated. It was found that the degradation follows pseudo-first-order and that SnS-coated copper cathode improves the efficiency of degradation, while SnS2-coated copper cathode decreases the efficiency of degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Sonochemistry is a sub-discipline of chemistry in which molecules undergo chemical reactions due to the application of powerful ultrasound radiation. The main events in such reactions are the creation, growth, and collapse of a bubble that is formed in the liquid. Bubbles grow through the diffusion of solute vapor into their volume. Then the collapse of a bubble occurs when it reaches its maximum size. One of many theories says that upon the collapse of a bubble very high temperatures (5000–25000 K) and pressures (order of magnitude 1000 atm) are obtained [1,2].

Sonochemistry is useful in the synthesis of nanoparticles of many simple (e.g. ZnS, CdS, PbSe, CuS) and complex chalcogenides (e.g. quaternary kesterite Cu2ZnSnS4) [3–5]. Sonochemical synthesis gives promise of effective large-scale production of low-cost nanoparticles under mild and environment-friendly conditions in contrast to strictly chemical methods, such as the solvothermal or the hot-injection methods, which involve high temperatures and organic solvents [4]. Further improvement of sonochemical synthesis can be achieved by its combination with electrochemistry [6]. Such hybrid approach is called sonoelectrochemistry, which allows to obtain nanomaterials with controlled sizes and shapes [7,8]. Recently sonochemical production of SnS and SnS2 nanoparticles in aqueous solution starting from respective tin chlorides (SnCl2 or SnCl4) has been widely reported [9–14]. SnS obtained by this method showed high photocatalytic activity in the photo-degradation of Methyl Blue and enhanced photovoltaic performance [9,10]. Sonochemically synthesized SnS2 nanoparticles showed antioxidant and antimicrobial properties [14]. Tin(II) sulfide is a very interesting material because of its electronic properties ideal for photovoltaics, photonics, and optoelectronics (solar cell devices, sensors, batteries etc.); however currently the best photovoltaic device based on SnS shows low efficiency of 4.36% [15,16]. Some of the most recent applications of tin sulfides include the detection of ethanol (SnS/SnS2 nanoparticles), anodes for lithium and sodium storage (SnS2 nanoparticle-integrated graphene nanosheets), and ultrafast broadband photodetector (SnS) [17 –19]. This study presents yet another application: modification of cathodes for the electro-Fenton process.

The electro-Fenton process, which belongs to advanced oxidation processes, is a good alternative to many methods of removing of synthetic dyes from wastewater, such as microbial fuel-cells, precipitation, coagulation, membrane separation, filtration, chemical oxidation and sonochemical treatment [20–25]. It provides efficient color removal and the destruction of pollutants molecules. The remediation of water contaminated with organic pollutants is very important issue because widely used in many industries synthetic azo-dyes can be carcinogenic both directly or due to the cleaved components [26]. Furthermore, non-toxic azo-dyes in the environment can be converted by microbes to hazardous, mutagenic, and carcinogenic compounds [27]. According to studies conducted on rats, azo-dye Metanil Yellow has neurotoxic, nephrotoxic, and hepatotoxic activity [28–30].

In the electro-Fenton process, H2O2 is generated in the reduction reaction of oxygen at the cathode [25]:
(1)
O 2
+ 2
H +
+ 2
e -
→
H 2
O 2

The hydrogen peroxide then reacts with the catalyst to produce hydroxyl radical [25]:
(2)
H
2
O
2
+
M
n +
→
M
n + 1
+
+
O
H
-
+
O
H
.
where M is the symbol of catalyst. Typically Fe2+ cations are used for that purpose, but many substitutes have been reported, such as Co2+, Mn2+, and Cu+ cations [25,31,32]. Also a concept of novel, two-electron catalysts such as Sn2+ and Bi3+ cations, was recently proposed and validated experimentally [33].

The consumed ions of catalyst are regenerated at the cathode in the reduction reaction [25]:
(3)
M
n + 1
+
+
e -
→
M
n +

The molecular oxygen necessary for the generation of hydrogen peroxide at the cathode is produced on the anode by oxidizing water in the reactor [25]:
(4)
2
H 2
O
→
4
H +
+
O 2
+
4
e -
Oxygen can be as well continuously passed through a reaction mixture, which is a better choice because of the low mass transport of generated oxygen on the anode.

Hydroxyl radicals then attack the molecule of dye and destroy it [34]:
(5)
Dye
+
O
H
.
→ →
P r o d u c t s

Rate constants of the reaction of the hydroxyl radical with aromatic hydrocarbons and various dyes are of 109–1010 M−1 s−1 order of magnitude, which means that this reaction is very fast [35,36]. However, the electro-Fenton process is a complex system of many reactions, and it was shown that the rate of dye degradation is limited by the rate of generation of OH∙ radicals, which is determined by the interplay between reactions (1), (2), and (3) [37].

Different cathodes and anodes have been applied in the electro-Fenton process, including the degradation of Metanil Yellow [38–40]. For example, a boron-doped diamond anode favors the production of hydroxyl radicals on its surface during water discharge, which improves the electro-Fenton process [38].

This paper presents sonochemical synthesis and characterization of SnS and SnS2 microparticles and application of them for modification of copper cathode in the degradation of model azo-dye Metanil Yellow by the electro-Fenton process. The kinetics and efficiency of this process are studied and compared for each cathode: copper, SnS-coated copper, and SnS2-coated copper. This is the main novelty of our study – such cathodes haven’t been studied before. Moreover, this is the first study of sonochemical syntheses of tin sulfides using ethanol and ethylenediamine as solvents, and starting from tin(II) chloride only.

All chemicals were pure for analysis (producer: Polskie Odczynniki Chemiczne). Solutions of H2SO4 (concentrated) and NaOH (1 M) were used. FeSO4·7H2O was used as the catalyst and Na2SO4·10 H2O was used to make supporting electrolyte. A commercial Metanil Yellow azo-dye (Fig. 1 ) was used in the present study without further purification. For sonochemical syntheses, SnCl2·2H2O and thioacetamide were used as reagents, and ethanol and ethylenediamine were used as solvents. For purification of obtained suspensions ethanol was used. Trichloromethane was used for the preparation of suspensions for UV–Vis spectrophotometry investigation. Copper plates were purchased from Polskie Odczynniki Chemiczne().

The sonochemical syntheses were conducted in conical flasks of 50 ml volume in an ultrasonic cleaner (Sonic-33 digital version, Polsonic) generating an ultrasound of 40 kHz frequency with power 2000 W. The acoustic power determined calorimetrically was 38 W/L. 20 ml of solvent was measured with a graduated cylinder and placed in a flask. As a solvents ethanol (in the synthesis of SnS2) and ethylenediamine (in the synthesis of SnS) were used. For each synthesis 451 mg of SnCl2·2H2O and 376 mg of thioacetamide were used. Weighed reagents were placed in the solvent in the conical flask and the mixture was stirred magnetically for 30 min. Then flasks were closed with glass stoppers and placed in the center of ultrasonic cleaner in the depth equal to the level of liquid in the flasks (Fig. 2 ). The amount of water in ultrasonic cleaner was 25 L. Ultrasound irradiation was applied for 100 min in the case of SnS, and for 160 min in the case of SnS2. The purification was carried out with the following procedure: after reaction, mixture was centrifuged and the supernatant was removed; then the sediment was suspended in fresh ethanol (20 ml) in the ultrasonic washer for 15 min; obtained suspension was centrifuged, supernatant was removed and the sediment was suspended in fresh ethanol (20 ml) in the ultrasonic washer for 15 min (this point was repeated 2 times). Finally, a suspension in ethanol is obtained. For the preparation of suspension in trichloromethane for UV–Vis measurements, the described above procedure of purification was applied using CHCl3, instead of ethanol, starting from suspension in ethanol.

The absorbance spectra of diluted transparent suspensions in CHCl3 of synthesized powders were recorded within the wavelength range of 400–1100 nm using a spectrophotometer Model Perkin-Elmer Lambda 20.

One drop of material/ethanol suspension was deposited on a standard TEM copper grid coated with an amorphous carbon film. Copper grids of this type are 30 nm thick and have a diameter of 3 mm. After the grid went completely dry it was ready to put into the microscope.

Specimen investigation was conducted on the FEI Titan Cubed 80–300 TEM with an accelerating voltage of 300 kV and the point resolution of 130 pm. The overview images were registered in bright-field TEM mode for magnifications ranging from 9.800× to 55.000×. For the purpose of high-resolution imaging magnifications from 145.000× − 380.000× range were used. In both cases, the images were obtained with the Gatan BM-Ultrascan CCD camera. STEM (scanning mode) images were acquired with the use of the HAADF detector which registers strongly scattered electrons. The in situ EDAX spectrometer was used for the analysis of the specimen's chemical composition.

The thin layer of synthesized powders of SnS and SnS2 were put on the conductive carbon type and introduced to the XPS spectrometer. XPS measurements were done using a Scienta R4000 hemispherical analyzer. The X-ray source was non-monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.7 eV). The wide spectra were registered with pass energy 500 eV and the step 0.5 eV. The narrow scans were acquired with pass energy 200 eV and the step 0.2 eV. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) for the Au 4f 7/2 line measured at the same experimental condition was equal to 1.1 eV. The energy scale was calibrated setting the C 1s line at the position 284.5 eV. Samples were measured as received.

The cathode was a copper plate of an area of 0.9 cm2 and the anode was a platinum plate of an area 11.5 cm2. The reactor was a beaker with a volume of 1 dm3. A pH-meter (CP-505, Elmetron) with pH electrode (ERH-13, Hydromet) and saturated calomel electrode (RU-10, Hydromet) (SCE) were also used in the present study. The position of each electrode was the same for every reaction. A Potentiostat Model EP 20 was used in the present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the electro-Fenton process and monitoring of dye degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure of conducting of the electro-Fenton reaction, as well as the procedure for monitoring of the dye degradation (including the construction of the calibration curve), is the same as in Matyszczak et al. (2019) [37].

Figs. 3 and 4
present diffractograms of sonochemically synthesized SnS and SnS2 powders. PXRD study in both cases suggests the obtaining of related phases, however, it shows poor crystallinity of obtained products. Synthesized powders have dark-brown (SnS) and honey (SnS2) colors.

The XPS wide scans confirmed that Sn, S, C, O and about 1 at % of Cl are present at samples surface. C, O originate from exposition to the air and from technology, Cl from used chemical compounds. The close examination of high resolution spectra of Sn 3d and S 2p showed that at the surface of powders is much less sulfur than in the bulk of formed microparticles studied by EDX in TEM (Table 1). The signal in XPS measurements is averaged from about 8.5 mm2. The analysis of Sn 3d and S 2p lines (Fig. 7 ) proved that in the compact SnS microparticles the binding energy (BE) of the line Sn 3d5/2 is 486.6 eV indicating the formation of Sn(II) (Fig. 7a). The binding energy of S 2p is 161.3 eV in agreement with that reported for SnS compound and S in the S-2 state [41] (Fig. 7b). In the case of SnS2 particles (Fig. 7 c and d), only about 15% of Sn is in Sn(IV) state as should be for the SnS2 compound (BE = 487.3 eV). The 85% is in Sn(II) states forming probably SnO at the surface of particles (BE = 486.5 eV). The binding energy and shape of S 2p is 161.3 eV like for S-2. Therefore, 15% of Sn is bonded with 31% of S giving stoichiometry close to 1:2 as in the SnS2 compound. The separation of the Sn 3d spin–orbit doublets in both component is 8.4 eV in agreement with XPS tables [42]. The binding energy of Sn with oxygen is the same like with sulfur [42]. Then, in the case of SnS 52% of Sn formed probably SnO.

As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 8 a), sonochemically obtained SnS2 particles form condensed, flower-like shapes. Oblong parts of clusters appear to have a crystalline structure. The width of those is not more than 3 nm as shown in Fig. 9 a. The crystallographic structure of said particles was determined as P-3m1. This result is in agreement with the PXRD result (Berndtite phase). Basing on fast-Fourier transform area 1 contains crystals aligned with the zone axis [1 0–1] and area 2 with the zone axis [1 0 0] (Fig. 10 ).

The HR-TEM image of sonochemically obtained SnS particles (Fig. 8b) shows that they appear as compact, globular formations. As it turned out during analysis these clusters are mainly amorphous. Highly oriented crystalline planes were found, their size is not more than 15 nm (as shown in Fig. 9b) but they appeared rarely. Due to that, it was not possible to determine the crystallographic structure of this material with satisfying reliability using fast-Fourier transform like in the case of SnS2 particles.

For more structural evidences, Fourier transforms of the high resolution images taken from the specimen region containing assembly of large amount of particles were compared with theoretical semi-rings based on XRD data (Fig. 11 ) [43,44].
The Tauc method yields 1.7 eV bandgap value for SnS powder and 2.3 eV for SnS2 powder. The corresponding plots are presented in Fig. 12 . Both bandgaps are direct.

The bandgap value of bulk-SnS2 is about 2.44 eV so the bandgap value of our tin(IV) sulfide powder looks realistic [45]. The difference can be due to the unconventional structure of these particles. The bandgap value of crystals of SnS is reported as 1.317 eV while our powder of tin(II) sulfide is characterized by 1.7 eV bandgap [46]. This can be mainly due to the formation of the SnO layer on the surface of SnS microparticles.

From a chemical point of view, it is interesting that starting from the same reagents – SnCl2 and thioacetamide – it is possible to obtain two different tin sulfides depending on which solvent is used. It seems that ethanol allows oxidation of Sn2+ cations to Sn4+ cations. Thioacetamide under ultrasound irradiation decomposes to form, among other products, H2S which then reacts with tin cations to form tin sulfides:
(6)
S
n
2 +
+
H 2
S
→
S n S
+
2
H +
(7)
S
n
4 +
+
2
H 2
S
→
S n
S 2
+
4
H +

On the other hand, ethylenediamine in some way protects the Sn2+ cations from being oxidized to Sn4+ cations. Moreover, the surface of both powders is covered in large amount by SnO with Sn2+ as resulted from XPS studies. It requires thorough studies to determine the detailed mechanism of this protection. It can be, hypothetically, due to the complexation of Sn2+ cations by ethylenediamine or due to different mechanisms of thioacetamide degradation in ethanol and in ethylenediamine. Moreover, the situation is more complicated because various byproducts can be formed during the whole process due to the presence of H∙ and OH∙ radicals in the reaction mixture.

Measured dye concentrations during electro-Fenton reactions were transformed according to the equation of the first-order kinetics:
ln C = -
k
obs
t + ln
C 0
where C is the dye concentration, C0 is the initial dye concentration, t is time and kobs is the observed kinetic constant. Linear regressions of the natural logarithm of dye concentration versus time were conducted using the RStudio package. Fig. 13 presents the experimental data for each cathode type. The experiments differ a bit in the initial concentration of Metanil Yellow due to the error of measuring volume by the graduated cylinder.
The observed kinetic constants and Pearson linear coefficients are shown in Table 2 .

An experimental study shows that SnS-coated copper cathode improves the performance (speed and efficiency) of the electro-Fenton process, while SnS2-coated copper cathode worsens this performance. The mean degradation efficiencies for each cathode are presented in Table 3 .

In the electro-Fenton process, there are two cathodic reactions: generation of hydrogen peroxide H2O2 (1) and the regeneration of catalyst (3). Enhancement of each of these reactions must improve the efficiency of the whole process because in the first case there will be more hydrogen peroxide and in the second case there will be faster regeneration of catalyst which means that there will be a faster generation of hydroxyl radicals OH∙ responsible of destruction of the dye molecule. Thus SnS microparticles may enhance one of these reactions or both at the same time, while SnS2 particles may diminish them.

SnS and SnS2 powders were obtained sonochemically starting from SnCl2 and thioacetamide. Using ethanol as a solvent leads to the formation of SnS2 while using ethylenediamine as a solvent leads to obtaining of SnS. XPS studies of both powders indicated the significant amount of SnO (tin(II)) at the powder surface beside the SnS (tin(II)) and SnS2 (tin(IV)). Synthesized tin(IV) sulfide particles showed flower-like morphology while tin(II) sulfide particles were rather spherical. The energy bandgaps of obtained products were estimated as 1.7 eV (for SnS) and 2.3 eV (for SnS2).

Copper cathodes coated with synthesized SnS and SnS2 powders have an influence on the degradation of model azo-dye Metanil Yellow in the electro-Fenton process. The pseudo-first-order kinetics of dye degradation is observed for each cathode type (copper, SnS-coated copper, and SnS2-coated copper). SnS-coated copper cathode (with Sn(II) only) increased the value of the observed kinetic constant while SnS2-coated copper cathode (with Sn(II) and Sn(IV)) decreased this value (in the comparison with pure copper cathode). The application of SnS-coated copper cathode leads to enhanced degradation efficiency in the electro-Fenton process. This founding gives a promise of further enhancement of electro-Fenton process performance by modification of cathode with other compounds. Furthermore, it would be interesting to study the potential influence of the powder synthesis method and its conditions on degradation efficiency.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Grzegorz Matyszczak: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis. Aleksandra Fidler: Investigation, Visualization. Emilia Polesiak: Investigation, Formal analysis. Małgorzata Sobieska: Investigation, Formal analysis. Krzysztof Morawiec: Investigation, Visualization. Wiktoria Zajkowska: Investigation, Visualization. Krystyna Lawniczak-Jablonska: Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Piotr Kuzmiuk: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
